speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us about these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, these pictures are-- were taken in Peoria, Illinois. and actually-- yeah, these were taken in Peoria, Illinois in 1948 when we first arrived, and it's my brother Al, my mother, and myself. And here I am, 136 pounds, also in the fall of 1948 in Peoria. And my mother and I at my mat mitzvah in Peoria. These are the two families that lived with us in our one little one bedroom apartment in Peoria. Here we are with-- here I am with my boyfriend Nathaniel. And-- of course, before we were married. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is at my-- after my graduation from high school, with my mother and my brother. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this all took place in-- in Peoria, Illinois, 1952, summer of '52. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] What a difference a few years make, huh? These are pictures of Nathaniel, my husband, at his high school graduation in 1949--

speaker: interviewee
text: '50. And this in my-- my yearbook 1953. And this is our wedding picture, August 2, 1953. Nathaniel and Marion Lazan. And here I am with Nathaniel and my brother Al and my mother. And here's a beautiful picture of my mother taken also in the early '50s in Peoria. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the way our house looks now in Hoya. My brother Al had gone back to Hoya a year and a half ago with his wife Diane. And there they also met very special people, Hans and [? Hika ?] [? Hute. ?] This is [? Hika ?] and her husband Hans. Hans is from Hoya, was born after the war, as was his wife, and his-- Hans's grandparents were friends of my grandparents. They're Gentiles, not Jewish. And they somehow kept in touch with my aunt, father's sister, Rosie, and indirectly, also kept in touch with my brother, Al. This is Diane, Al's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: This trip was in 1993?

speaker: interviewee
text: '93 actually, yes. And it was prior to '93 that we had gotten to know Hans and [? Hika ?] [? Hute. ?] I just want to go back to this particular picture, which was taken in June of 1945. As I said, in June of 1945. It is the grave of my father the way we left it when we left Trobitz. And the way my-- my brother buried my father. We did not know from the time that we left there whether there would be anything left of the grave whatsoever. In 1991, my mother received a manila envelope and-- from Hans and [? Hika ?] [? Hute. ?] In it were pictures of a most dignified Jewish cemetery, which they had taken after Germany was reunited in 1990. They took it upon-- upon themselves to travel to Trobitz, to see what happened to the grave, and found that this dignified cemetery was built with a fence around it and the Star of David interspersed. And the stone on my father's grave had his name on it, the date of his birth, and the date he died. It was the most amazing and-- thing that-- that-- that we had seen, and my mother was just overwhelmed. Here all along we thought there was nothing left of this very primitive grave at the time when it was-- this picture was taken. So we're forever grateful to [? Hikes-- ?] Hans and [? Hika ?] [? Hute ?] who also took it upon themselves to have a booklet published about the Jewish families on Hoya. They themselves-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- it was there-- it was from their expenses from which they had these booklets published and made sure that are distributed in the schools and surrounding area of Hoya and in Hoya itself. They felt it was important for the children and for all of the people there to know what had happened to the six, seven families-- Jewish families in their hometown of Hoya. And they came and they interviewed my mother, and my brother, and me, and my entire story is in this book. And it's a-- a wonderful, wonderful deed that these people did. And we're very grateful to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you hold that open, that page?

speaker: interviewee
text: Open it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Looking at the photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact-- whoops, that's all right. This is a list of the people who are buried in Trobitz, and there is my father's name, Walter Blumenthal. And nationality is stateless, which we were, and the date that he was born, and the data that he died. And then we have [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] my brother, and my father, and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you hold it for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hold the page with your brother, father, and mother open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hold it for 5 more seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the cemetery. And then here is a picture. And this is interspersed in my story. My husband and I. Nathaniel and I. And here-- do we show this later, the newspaper clipping?

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about it right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is a picture that was on the front page of the Long Island taken in April of '91 where my story was told inside. And it's just a-- a remarkable thing that these Gentile people did to, I guess, to make themselves feel better too, and to to let the younger generation know as to what had actually happened in Hoya to the Jewish community there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, want me to take this for the moment? I'll put it here so you can hold that page up. And bend it as much as you can, so we can get the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Make it exactly perpendicular. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, this-- this is where you have to focus--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Al.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, where? If you hold it, we can even go-- if you look at the whole frame. Could you explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a book on Westerbork, which was printed, obviously, after the war, and sometime in the '50s. And here is a picture that was taken in Westerbork at Hanukkah time in the 1940s, early '40s. And there is my brother, Al. This same exact picture is also shown in the Encyclopedia Judaica under Westerbork, and in a few other publications. And it is just a coincidence that my brother Al was depicted right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you show the cover of the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cover is It's-- it's in Dutch saying, it's the camp of hope and despair, from 1939 to 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? Yeah, it's good this way. This is a Siddur, a prayer book, that my father had with him throughout his time in the concentration camps, in Westerbork, as well as in Bergen-Belsen. It obviously got-- got wet, and it-- it went through some very hard times. He used it whenever he possibly could. He became very religious in camp, and he always was a very observant, but became more so in camp. And he used this prayer book whenever possible. And let me put this down. And in this bag, which is a-- a tefillin bag which my mother embroidered for my father, prior in Westerbork or prior to that. It has his tefillin in it, and he also, whenever possible, would [? lay ?] tefillin and say the morning prayer. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I think, before we get--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a very interesting piece of mail which my father wrote to my mother from Buchenwald in 1938, November 12, 1938. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In it, you can see Buckenwald, and he wrote and was told to write very legible. It is stated up here on top in German that it had to be written legibility without complaints, and otherwise it would be censored and it would not be allowed to be sent. And it was a very fairly satisfying piece of mail for my mother to receive, even though she knew that it was sort of forced writing on my father's part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please turn it over and hold it very steady.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK. I'd like to introduce my family. [LAUGHS] This was taken at Yo'av brit milah in Cincinnati, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family-- family picture taken at Yo'av's brit milah in June of 1993 and in Cincinnati, Ohio. This is my husband Nathaniel, my daughter Susan, daughter-in-law Lisa, and our David, and I. This is my mother, Ruth Meyberg with our eldest grandchild Arielle And this is Michael and his wife Rachel with [? Gavriel ?] and Dalia, and this is baby Yo'av. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What would you like me to move to?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anywhere you want, just tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a David, who lives with his wife, Lisa, in Vero Beach, Florida. He's a urologist and Lisa is an attorney. Here is my mother and myself. This was just taken December 20, 1994, just a few-- just a month or so ago. And this is our grandson [? Gavriel ?] who is six and a half. His sister Dalia, who's three and a half. And Yo'av, who's now one and a half. All three children live in Israel, in Ra'anana. This is a picture of our eldest, Arielle, and her cousin Gavi. And this is Arielle's younger brother, our Joshua, who is four years old. Arielle, by the way, is seven. And Gavi is six and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is our Susan and her husband Rob. Rob is a physical therapist, and Susan is an expert in computer and a chef. And this is Arielle. And Arielle and Joshua. And this is a picture of our-- am I going to fast? A picture of our six children, taken at the time of Arielle's birth. This was really about six, seven years ago. Michael and Rachel. Michael and Rachel moved to Isabel in August with their three children. And he's with [? Neka, ?] which is a very important soap and cosmetic company in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Nathaniel, and I, and our eldest, David, and his wife Lisa, Susan, Rob, and Arielle as a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you had mentioned before that you started in about 1979 talking in schools, and that there was a special story you wanted to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell that now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do want to share a very special story with you. I have been speaking regularly, every spring, in schools and for organizations, and in fact my very first time of speaking at a school was at a junior high school in Brooklyn, and I assumed it was going to be a classroom situation, but when I arrived at the school, I was brought into an auditorium, and there were about 200 to 300 junior high school students. I was told to that the children might get restless and get up and leave and may not be interested in what I had to say. But the woman in charge of the social studies department felt it was very important to introduce the Holocaust in the curriculum and felt it was much more impressive to have someone speak about it as opposed to show a film or giving them reading-- giving the children reading material. And that's when I came in. And a friend of mine, [? Pearl ?] [? Lipton, ?] asked me to speak and to do the part, and I said of course I would, with trepidations, because it is always most difficult to make the preparations, to think about it, and than to-- to talk about it. And even though I know how important it is to share the-- my background-- and-- and I let the children know that it is difficult and-- Anyway, the time came, and I came to the auditorium. There they were, 200 or 300 children. And my mother had been here, was here at that time from San Francisco. And she was sitting in-- in the back of the auditorium with a few of my friends, and she asked not to be involved. When I was finished talking, and even while I was talking, you could hear a pin drop. It was absolutely amazing how the children paid attention, and how attentive they were. And I could tell by the questions that were asked towards the end that they listened carefully.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- in my talk, I invariably and always give all the credit of our survival to my mother. And this, in itself, must have made an enormous impression on the children, because the very first question asked after I was through speaking was where my mother was at this time? And I told the children that she was sitting in the back, and that she was here from San Francisco.

speaker: interviewee
text: This 11, 12, 13-year-old boy stood up and applauded her. And the others all followed suit. It was the most heartwarming experience, and it was just-- just really a wonderful, wonderful, rewarding feeling for me to see that these children understood what my story was all about. And I believe they identified, because they were pretty much my age at the time when I experienced my life in camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which school this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a school in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Heidi-- ?] [? Heidi ?] something junior high school? I can look it up. And invariably people do ask whether I have written anything, written it down, whether I wrote a book. A book will be published by Greenwillow. It's a division of William Morrow, and it'll be published early part of next year. And I wrote this with Lila Perl who was a-- a wonderful lady, and has written some 50 books of her own. And I just feel it is of utmost importance to share my story and everyone else's story who's-- who's gone through life and-- during the Holocaust and there are really no two stories alike, and it doesn't matter whether you've gone through the same camps and experienced the same situations-- everyone perceives it so differently, and therefore, the stories are always different, and they must be shared. And-- and they must be told and retold.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. Now we'll meet you family. OK, please introduce your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I start with my husband, Nathaniel. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: How about your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I start with my husband, Nathaniel. And-- and this is my mom, Ruth Meyberg, who will be 87 years old tomorrow, February 7. This is the lady that I talk so much about, and I'm mighty, mighty proud to be her daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're just very, very grateful to have her here with us. And my daughter Susan, and-- who is just the dearest daughter of all. She's just a wonderful, wonderful human being and a very, very dear person, as are her siblings, David, and Lisa, and Michael, and Rachel. And her husband Rob is a wonderful son-in-law, sensitive, and a wonderful human being. And this is our eldest Arielle Ruth Weinberg, [? Nathania ?] Arielle Ruth in Hebrew, named after my mother-in-law, another wonderful person and after Rob's father. [? Nathan-- ?] [? Nessen. ?] And that's where the [? Nathania ?] comes in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Joshua Norman Weinberg, Norman named after Rob's father. And Joshua is-- how old are you Joshua? Joshua is four years old, and Arielle is seven years old. And we are so very grateful to have our grandchildren here with us, and we're grateful for our grandchildren in Israel as well. That's Gavi, Dalia, and Yo'av, and we wish they could-- they could be here with us right now as well. And-- Is there anyone else would like to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll say a word about Marion. Sometimes you don't too many good, major decision in one's lifetime. I made a very good one, back October 10, 1952 when I asked Marion to walk her home from shul after Yom Kippur. And I think we started going steadily [INAUDIBLE] the next night. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents said, why don't you wait till after the-- after the Air Force to get married? And then they said, why don't you wait until after college to get married? We were married between my junior and senior year in college, and we went to school as a married couple, which was challenging. I had to keep up with her good marks. And we both worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: She taught Sunday school. I worked part time in a department store. And then she was very good to me in the Air Force. Learning to fly is not so easy, and it was difficult, and she gave me that courage that I needed, that-- that moral strength which saw me through to get my silver wings. And while we were in the Air Force, two of our children were born, David and Susan. But Marion always does things a little-- a little differently. And between David and  Susan, Marion was a polio victim in the Clinton County in Texas, the 11th victim that year, according to the news reports, but she recovered and went on. She was a great lady in Texas, helping out in the hospitals. And one of things she does in the community-- she is very good with people in need, people who have a hurt, people who have a problem. And she knows how to listen to people. She knows how to soothe-- soothe people whether it's family or complete strangers. She has that knack. She's been very active in the community. She's been sisterhood president, she's been a PTA.

speaker: interviewee
text: Medical assistant.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's been a medical assistant to a doctor. [? Kava, ?] here in Valley Stream, who just retired. He'll be 90 on Saint Patrick's Day. And she's been with him 12 and a half years. She's a spectacular wife, wonderful friend. She's a caring person who cared about my parents, about my mom, and my dad. And she is with my sister-- they're almost like sisters. There's no difference. As far as she is as a mother, only Susan can tell. Susan?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's one thing that you left out-- is that for somebody who's gone through the hell that my mother has, and my grandmother has, there's no bitterness. And she's very understanding towards other people. And somebody who has gone through something like that, can very certainly be bitter and have a negative attitude towards life. And we don't see that at all. And we all try to live up to her standards, which is not always easy, but she tells us not to worry about it. And she's just-- she a great safta to my kids. And she's a great daughter to my grandmother, and she's a great mother to me. And I'm sure she's a great wife to my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything for your safta?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I guess that's it. Mom? My mother-in-law also is a very strong person--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank god-- well, in addition to [? mama, ?] without my mother-- mother-in-law's perseverance, there would no Marion here, and then there would be none of us. And-- and one of the ironic things about all of this is that they [? had ?] [? a ?] [? plane ?] [? ticket ?] to Palestine during the camp. They almost went there after camp. And here, Michael, the youngest, decided on his own to go to Israel. And that's after my mother-in-law had written, but never mailed, a very strong letter about leaving this blessed United States of America, which she said nobody should take for granted. None of us should take for granted. It is a wonderful country. But, as Michael said, there has to be an Israel. And as Marion said earlier in the interview, if there were an Israel, maybe a few million more people would have survived, so it's how things go around in circles all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is this enough now? Can we get up?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you very much for giving us the opportunity to share my story with you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's our-- our pleasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You guys are usually much more talkative than what you are.
